explanation: The Sigma rule explicitly checks for the string '-GetCACAPS' in the 'selection_cli' condition. Since Windows utilities typically accept both hyphens and forward slashes for command line switches, substituting it with '/GetCACAPS' successfully bypasses this exact string match.

The 'selection_ip' regex strictly looks for the standard dotted-decimal IPv4 format (e.g., X.X.X.X). By converting the target IP address (192.168.1.100) to its Decimal/Integer representation (3232235876), the underlying Windows network APIs will still resolve and connect to it, but the SIEM's regular expression will fail to match.

Similar to the decimal encoding technique, converting the IPv4 address into its Hexadecimal equivalent (0xC0A80164) completely evades the regex expecting numbers separated by periods, while the tool still successfully downloads the file.

By using an IPv4-mapped IPv6 address format, the URL syntax fundamentally changes. The regex defined in 'selection_ip' expects the sequence '://' directly followed by a number, so the presence of the IPv6 brackets and prefix completely breaks the detection.
Bypasses the selection_ip regex by using the decimal integer representation of the IP address (e.g., 2886729738 instead of 172.16.0.10) rather than the standard dotted-quad notation.
Bypasses the selection_ip regex by utilizing a fully qualified domain name (FQDN) to host the payload rather than a direct IP address.

Bypasses the selection_cli condition by using CMD caret (^) obfuscation to break up the '-GetCACAPS' string, preventing the exact string match while remaining functional in the Windows command prompt.
